concepts.title: Government Budget is a comprehensive financial statement
concepts.description: Government budget is a comprehensive financial statement that records all the income and expenditure of the government during a particular period, usually a year.

concepts.title: Government Budget is a tool for economic management
concepts.description: Government budget is a tool for economic management that helps in achieving the objectives of economic growth, employment, price stability, and distribution of income.

concepts.title: Types of Government Budget
concepts.description: There are three types of government budget: (1) Revenue Budget, (2) Capital Budget, and (3) Fiscal Budget.

concepts.title: ARISE
concepts.description: ARISE is a mnemonic to remember the purposes of government budget: Achievement of economic growth, Reduction of inequality, Increase in employment, Stabilization of prices, and Employment generation.

concepts.title: Revenue Budget and Capital Budget are the same
concepts.description: Revenue Budget and Capital Budget are two different types of government budgets. Revenue Budget records the income and expenditure of the government for a particular year, whereas Capital Budget records the investment in capital assets such as buildings, roads, and industries.

concepts.title: Deficit Budget
concepts.description: A deficit budget is a budget in which the government's expenditure exceeds its revenue. It is also known as a fiscal deficit.

concepts.title: Fiscal Policy is a part of Government Budget
concepts.description: Fiscal policy is a part of government budget that deals with the use of government spending and taxation to achieve economic objectives such as economic growth, employment, and price stability.

concepts.title: Government Budget can be either Balanced or Unbalanced
concepts.description: Government budget can be either balanced or unbalanced. A balanced budget is one in which the government's revenue equals its expenditure, whereas an unbalanced budget is one in which the government's expenditure exceeds its revenue.
